note: Mathlib/Topology/Sion.lean:336-340, `theorem minimax` proves inf_sup = sup_inf (Sion's minimax equality) for quasiconcave/quasiconvex functions on convex sets. File docstring: 'As a particular case, one get the von Neumann theorem where f is bilinear and E, F are finite dimensional' — a strict generalization of the classical von Neumann minimax theorem the Wikidata 'Minimax' article centers on, not a specialization of it.
verify_note: Q751319 'Minimax' is Wikidata's broad decision-rule/algorithm concept spanning AI game-tree search, statistics, philosophy, and voting (P31=theorem+algorithm; en.wiki intro: 'a decision rule used in artificial intelligence, decision theory, combinatorial game theory, statistics, and philosophy') -- it is NOT centered on the von Neumann minimax theorem as the proposer's note asserts, and Wikidata carries separate, more precise QIDs for that (Q913849 'minimax theorem', Q7525341 'Sion's minimax theorem') that this highly technical topological decl should target instead; as filed to Q751319, 'generalization' overclaims Sion.minimax's relation to the whole broad decision-rule concept.

note: Mathlib/MeasureTheory/Integral/IntervalIntegral/TrapezoidalRule.lean:32-34, `noncomputable def trapezoidal_integral` plus `trapezoidal_error_le` (convergence/error bound, line 18) formalize the trapezoidal rule, one specific numerical-integration method. Decl already carries @[wikidata Q833293] (Trapezoidal rule); it is a narrower special case of the broader 'Numerical integration' field concept, for which no general quadrature/Simpson's-rule/Monte-Carlo-integration decl exists in Mathlib.
verify_note: trapezoidal_integral (TrapezoidalRule.lean:34) and trapezoidal_error_le (verified to actually live at line 226, not the docstring-mention line 18 the note cites, but the theorem is real) formalize the trapezoidal rule; decl's existing @[wikidata Q833293] tag correctly resolves to 'trapezoidal rule'/'numerical integration method', and Q753445 'Numerical integration' is confirmed on Wikidata as 'family of algorithms for finding the definite integral of a function' -- trapezoidal_integral is honestly one specific method (special_case) within that family, not an exact match or a generalization.
